actual_globe_prediction: 3,291 km geodesic, confirmed by direct flights (~3,290 km great-circle distance). The 3,287 km figure the dome cites is the standard WGS84 geodesic — both values agree. The dome presents this as a disagreement, but its own 4,352 km matches a circuitous railway, not a geometric distance.
our_finding: V13 Finsler formula was calibrated against the Indian Pacific railway distance (not a blind prediction), matches a circuitous rail route rather than the geometric city-pair distance, and fails by 78% on Sydney-Buenos Aires in V12. Two internal methods (Finsler vs MDS scaffold) disagree by 460 km on Sydney-Perth itself.
reproducibility: Partially reproducible. The Indian Pacific distance is public record (4,352 km track length). The globe geodesic (3,291 km) is independently verifiable via flight distances. The dome's V13 formula is partially published but the n(r) scaling function is not, preventing independent replication of the Finsler calculation.
kernel_of_truth: The dome's V13 Finsler coordinate system is genuinely more sophisticated than naive flat-earth azimuthal equidistant projections. It uses elliptic integrals, two-zone topology, and position-dependent refraction — real mathematical tools applied to a problem most flat-earth models ignore entirely. Credit for engaging with the hardest problem rather than hiding from it.

actual_globe_prediction: ~35.9° ± measurement uncertainty. Globe predicts altitude ≈ latitude ± corrections for Polaris polar offset (±0.66°), atmospheric refraction (~0.02°), and instrument precision (±0.2–0.5° for field sextant). The claimed 0.27° excess is well within these known error sources.
our_finding: The claimed +0.27° excess is smaller than Polaris's known polar offset (±0.66°), within typical field measurement uncertainty (±0.2–0.5°), and is based on a single unreplicated measurement with no published methodology. The dome's own site marks its broader Polaris-latitude prediction as FALSIFIED (FAIL-001), creating an internal contradiction.
reproducibility: Not reproducible. No instrument, methodology, time of observation, atmospheric conditions, or number of measurements published. A single unreplicated measurement cannot be independently verified.
kernel_of_truth: Polaris IS offset from the true celestial pole by ~0.66° (currently ~0.66° and precessing). This means its altitude does vary with hour angle, and simplified altitude=latitude statements in introductory astronomy are slightly imprecise. The dome is correct to demand precision in this measurement.

actual_globe_prediction: Eastbound advantage of ~55–75 minutes (varying by season), caused by the polar jet stream. Standard atmospheric science predicts: larger asymmetry in winter, smaller in summer, absent near equator, and variable by hemisphere. All four confirmed by data.
our_finding: Three independent discriminating tests — seasonal variation, equatorial absence, and southern hemisphere pattern — all match jet stream physics and are incompatible with a fixed aetheric circulation. The dome's AI context page (Rule 15, added between V50.6 and V51.1) confirms relabeling, not a new mechanism.
reproducibility: Fully reproducible. Airline schedules, OpenSky ADS-B data, and peer-reviewed studies (Williams 2016) provide independent confirmation.
kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies that transatlantic flight time asymmetry is large, consistent, and sometimes undersold in casual explanations. The ~13% eastbound advantage on JFK-LHR is a striking real-world phenomenon.
straw_man_detail: The dome frames the globe prediction as 0% (after wind) — implying the jet stream is a correction to be subtracted. The jet stream IS the explanation, not a correction.

dome_prediction: Below 21,500 nT by December 2026 (PRED-R002: ≤21,750 nT by end 2028)
actual_globe_prediction: Continued SAA deepening. IGRF-14, CHAOS-7, and WMM2025 all predict ongoing field decay. Aubert (2015) forecasts 1.46 ± 0.4 μT surface decrease by 2065. Globe models predict the same direction — decline.
our_finding: Non-discriminating: both models predict continued SAA weakening. The dome extrapolates from CHAOS-7 station decay rates — globe observational data. A spreadsheet produces the same prediction.
reproducibility: Fully reproducible. INTERMAGNET station data is public. CHAOS-7, IGRF-14, and WMM2025 models are published.
kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies the SAA two-cell structure and commits to specific falsifiable thresholds — commendable methodology even though the prediction is non-unique.
straw_man_detail: The dome claims the globe predicts ~21,800 nT (stability). Every major geomagnetic model predicts continued decline, not stability.

dome_prediction: −28.9 nT at Ebro station (killshot.html). Note: homepage cites −17 to −21 nT; monitor.py computes −29.1 nT. Three different values for the same prediction.
actual_globe_prediction: 5–25 nT magnetic perturbation via Chapman ionospheric mechanism (peer-reviewed since 1933). Confirmed quantitatively across dozens of eclipses.
our_finding: Chapman mechanism predicts 5–25 nT, fully explaining the dome's range. Eclipse path requires JPL DE440/441 ephemerides (globe Keplerian orbits). Three inconsistent dome values (−17 to −21, −28.9, −29.1 nT) undermine claimed precision.
reproducibility: Directly testable August 12, 2026. INTERMAGNET Ebro data is public. Any result in 5–30 nT confirms both models.
kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies that solar eclipses produce measurable magnetic perturbations. Committing to a specific station (Ebro) and including a Kp<2 precondition is scientifically sound methodology.
straw_man_detail: The dome claims the globe predicts 0 nT. Chapman published the ionospheric eclipse magnetic mechanism in 1933. The 0 nT claim is flatly false.

dome_prediction: NMP drift rate <20 km/yr by 2027 (deceleration constant 0.08)
dome_claimed_globe_prediction: Continued high-speed drift (implying no deceleration predicted)
actual_globe_prediction: NMP deceleration documented in mainstream literature. Livermore et al. (2020, Nature Geoscience) attributed acceleration to a hydromagnetic jet now weakening. WMM2025 and IGRF-14 incorporate the deceleration trend.
dome_status: Pending (killshot.html shows Pending; internal tracking shows 39.9% error — asymmetric scorecard)
our_finding: The dome's internal tracking reports 39.9% error — exceeding the dome's own 30% falsification threshold. Current drift ~35 km/yr. Observed deceleration ~1 km/yr/yr over 2003–2024. Reaching 20 km/yr by end 2027 requires ~9 km/yr/yr deceleration — 9× the historical rate. The prediction is not just currently failing; the required course correction is physically implausible.
reproducibility: Fully reproducible. NOAA publishes NMP position data. The dome's own tracking page reports the 39.9% error. WMM2025 provides independent drift rate estimates.
kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies NMP deceleration — real geomagnetic science confirmed by WMM2025. Credit for maintaining a tracking page that honestly reports 39.9% error rather than hiding it.
straw_man_detail: The dome claims the globe predicts continued high-speed drift. WMM2025 and IGRF-14 both incorporate the deceleration trend. Livermore et al. (2020) published the mechanism in Nature Geoscience.

dome_prediction: All stars show identical parallax of ~0.454 arcsec (from 20m firmament wobble)
actual_globe_prediction: Parallax ∝ 1/distance; range from 0.001 arcsec (distant stars) to 769 arcsec (Proxima Centauri). Gaia DR3 measured 1.8 billion stars and found 760× variation in parallax tracking distance.
our_finding: The dome predicts all stars on a firmament at fixed distance ~20,015 km should show the same annual wobble from a 20m firmament oscillation: ~0.454 arcsec. Gaia DR3 measured parallax ranging from <0.001 to 769 arcsec — a 760× range tracking inverse distance. The dome prediction is rejected at 6,281σ for Proxima Centauri alone. No post-hoc refraction adjustment can produce 760× parallax variation from a single-distance firmament.
reproducibility: Fully reproducible. Gaia DR3 catalog is public (ESA, 2022). The 6,281σ figure uses Gaia's quoted parallax and uncertainty for Proxima Centauri (768.7 ± 0.1 mas). Cross-checks available from Hipparcos catalog and ground-based interferometry.
kernel_of_truth: Annual parallax IS a real effect caused by Earth's orbital motion — the dome author correctly understands that parallax should exist. The error is in predicting uniform parallax from a firmament rather than distance-proportional parallax from actual stellar distances.
